segments.s538-g25: فرستاد is a past-tense verb 'sent/dispatched' (third-person). بر here is the preposition 'toward/to' and precedes its noun phrase; Persian often places direction phrases before or after the verb. هر سوی لشکری is a noun phrase 'to/for every quarter/side, an army' where هر modifies سوی and لشکری ('troops, an army') is the object of the preposition.
segments.s538-g26: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('that/where'); هرجا که باشد is a fixed conditional/subjunctive pattern meaning 'wherever it may be' (باشد is classical subjunctive/present form 'be/may be'). ز here is the poetic form of از ('from/of'), and دشمن سری literally 'an enemy head' (سری = 'head/chief'), i.e. referring to the presence/location of an enemy.
segments.s538-g27: سر is the direct object 'head' (here literal: decapitated head). کینه‌ورشان is a plural noun with the possessive suffix -شان 'their [spiteful ones/foes]'; Persian allows the possessor as a clitic. به راه آورید is an imperative plural ('bring to the road/put on display'); the object (سر کینه‌ورشان) precedes the verb, which is common in literary Persian.
segments.s538-g28: گر is the conditional 'if'. آیین شمشیر is a genitive/nominal compound 'the custom/practice of the sword' i.e. warfare as a practice. و links گاه ('time/appointed moment') as another element tied to the verb; آورید is the repeated imperative 'bring' applying across the coordinate structure, so the line says 'if warfare is the custom, bring [them] at the proper time.'
segments.s538-g17: نباید که is an impersonal negative: literally 'it should not be that...' (a prohibition or ruling). از introduces the group 'from/among', and کارداران من means 'my administrators/agents': کارداران is a plural noun 'officials, managers' and من is the possessor ('my'). The clause is a left-hand fragment that will be completed by the following phrase.
segments.s538-g18: ز here (= از) is the poetic preposition 'from/among'. سرهنگ و جنگی سواران من is a coordination: 'colonels/commanders and warlike horsemen (my mounted warriors)'; جنگی is an adjective modifying سواران. من again marks possession ('my'). The phrase completes the restriction introduced by نباید که.
segments.s538-g19: بخسپد is a rare/archaic form here meaning roughly 'be at rest / be satisfied' (the morphology is optative/subjunctive-like), followed by کسی 'anyone' as the subject and دل پر از آرزوی 'a heart full of desire' as the object/phrase describing that person. Word order places the verb first in this poetic line, and پر از is the standard 'full of' construction.
segments.s538-g20: گر = 'if'. از introduces the following agent/type. بنده‌گر is an agentive/occupational noun (literally 'one who binds or enslaves' in this context), followed by مردم 'people' and نیک‌خوی 'kind‑natured / gentle' describing disposition. Together the conditional restricts the preceding wish: even if he is such a person (an enslaver) who is kind by nature.
segments.s538-g21: گشادست is a predicative adjective with the archaic copular suffix -ست: 'is wide/open'. The following prepositional phrase بر هرکس ('to/for everyone') marks the beneficiary, and this بارگاه ('this court') is the topic; word order is topical/poetic but reads 'this court is open to everyone.'
segments.s538-g22: ز ... وز is a paired construction meaning 'from ... and from ...' (the poet uses ز + وز for euphony). بدخواه = 'ill‑wisher' and مردم نیک‌خواه = 'people who wish well' (well‑wishers). The phrase stresses that the court is open alike to friends and enemies.
segments.s538-g23: همه انجمن is the subject 'the entire assembly' (همه + انجمن). خواندند is a past plural verb 'they cried/sang/recited' and takes آفرین as its direct object: they voiced the acclamation. Note the plural verb agrees with 'انجمن' viewed collectively.
segments.s538-g24: که introduces the content of the acclamation: آباد بادا is an optative formula 'may it be prosperous' (بادا = 'may it be'). به دادت combines به ('by/through') with دادت (here 'your justice', a nominal use) and زمین 'the land/earth': 'May the land be prosperous through your justice.' This is an archaic, formulaic blessing.
segments.s538-g9: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/for'), اندر is a poetic_LOCATIVE preposition 'in', and جهان is the clause subject 'the world'. داد is a noun 'justice' (not a verb here) and گنج is another noun 'treasure' that forms the phrase 'دادِ گنج' (the treasure that is justice). منست is an archaic/poetic copula (older spelling/contraction like من است) that predicates the phrase — 'is mine / belongs to me' / 'is my ...' — so the word order is conjunction + locative + subject + noun phrase + copula.
segments.s538-g10: This is a second clause linked to the first; جهان is subject, زنده is a predicate adjective 'alive/living'. از introduces source/reason ('by/through/because of') and takes the coordinated phrase بخت و رنج 'fortune and suffering' as the source; منست is again the copula/predicative ending. Word order is subject + predicate + source phrase + copula.
segments.s538-g11: کس here is negative 'no one' (preceding the object); این گنج is the direct object 'this treasure'. نتواند is the negative potential form of توانستن ('cannot' — third person), followed by the verb ستد (from ستاندن/ستاندن 'to take/seize') as the action that cannot be done; از من is the source/agent 'from me'. The order is emphatic: subject/negative quantifier + object + modal verb + source + verb.
segments.s538-g12: بد آید is an impersonal/experiential construction 'bad/ill comes' meaning 'evil befalls' or 'ill happens'. به مردم marks the experiencers/recipients 'to the people'. ز is the poetic variant of از ('from/because of') introducing the cause 'کردار بد' (bad deed/conduct). The clause thus reads 'ill happens to the people because of bad conduct.'
segments.s538-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خشنود باشد uses the present/subjunctive form باشد to describe the state 'is pleased' (چو + subjunctive yields a temporal clause). جهاندار is the noun 'sovereign/ruler' and پاک is an adjective modifying it ('pure'/'righteous'). The order places the temporal marker before the predicate and its subject.
segments.s538-g14: ندارد is the negative form of داشتن 'does not have'. دریغ ('reluctance/pity') is its object. از من indicates the target/relatum 'toward me' or 'in regard to me'. این تیره خاک is a nominative/ topical phrase placed after the verb for poetic inversion ('this dark earth'). Together: 'this dark earth has no reluctance toward me.'
segments.s538-g15: جهان is the subject 'the world'. سر به سر is an idiomatic, repetitive phrase meaning 'all over' or 'completely' (literally 'head to head/top to top'). در پناه is a prepositional phrase 'in the protection/of the shelter' and منست is the poetic copula (older/manuscript form) 'is (mine / belongs to me / is under me)'. Word order follows poetic inversion but reads 'the whole world is in my protection.'
segments.s538-g16: پسندیدن is a verbal noun/masdar here ('liking/approval' or 'to approve'). داد ('justice') follows in genitive relation (the liking of justice). راه is the noun 'way/path' and منست is the copula/predicative ending — 'the approval of justice is my way.' The phrase uses the gerund as subject with a possessed noun following it.
segments.s538-g1: This clause places the locative prepositional phrase 'به بغداد' (to/into Baghdad) at the front for setting the scene, followed by the past tense verb 'بنشست' (he sat/settled). The phrase 'بر تخت عاج' is a preposition ('بر' = on) + noun ('تخت' = throne) + modifier ('عاج' = ivory) where the modifier follows the noun, a typical Persian noun phrase order.
segments.s538-g2: 'به سر برنهاد' is a light-prepositional phrase ('به سر' = on his head) followed by the past verb 'برنهاد' (placed on). The demonstrative 'آن' precedes the adjective-noun pair 'دلفروز تاج' (an epithetical adjective + noun), a poetic ordering that yields 'that heart-illuminating crown'.
segments.s538-g3: 'کمر بسته' is a participial/adjectival phrase (literally 'waist bound') meaning 'having girded his waist' — Persian often uses past-participle adjectives after the noun. The coordination 'و' links the next nominal phrase 'گرز شاهان به دست' where 'گرز شاهان' is a genitive construction ('the mace of kings') and 'به دست' indicates 'in (his) hand'.
segments.s538-g4: The word 'بیاراسته' here functions as a past-participle/adjectival verb meaning 'having set/arranged/adorned' (an archaic/poetic usage), taking 'جایگاه' (place/seat) as its object; the final 'نشست' is a simple past verb 'he sat'. The sequence shows a preparatory action followed by the resulting sitting.
segments.s538-g5: The verb 'خواندند' here means 'they called/summoned/named' and takes 'شهنشاه' as the name/title given. The adverbial 'زان پس' ('thereafter/from that then') modifies the following clause, and 'ورا' is the unstressed object pronoun 'him'. The word order (Title + verb + temporal adverb + object) is a poetic reordering but grammatically clear.
segments.s538-g6: The preposition 'ز' ('from/of') introduces the relation to 'گشتاسپ' (Gushtasp). The verb here (poetic form) indicates lack of recognition, with 'کس' ('anyone/no one') as the subject and 'ورا' ('him') as object — the poetic word order places the prepositional phrase first ('ز گشتاسپ').
segments.s538-g7: The subordinating conjunction 'چو' here means 'when' and introduces a temporal clause; 'تاج بزرگی' places the noun 'تاج' (crown) before its qualifying nominal adjective 'بزرگی' (greatness/ grandeur), and the past verb 'برنهاد' finishes the clause ('placed on'). This is a standard temporal clause + past action structure.
segments.s538-g8: 'چنین کرد' ('thus he did') is a common resumptive phrase meaning 'he acted in this way'; the prepositional phrase 'بر تخت پیروزه' ('upon the turquoise throne') locates the action and 'یاد' ('remembrance/memorial') is the result or object of that action — poetically 'he made the throne a remembrance'.

sentences.s538-s13: He dispatched troops in every direction, wherever an enemy might be.
sentences.s538-s14: Bring the heads of their grudge‑bearers out onto the road; if the custom is the sword, bring them at the appointed time.
sentences.s538-s9: It ought not to be that, among my officials — whether commanders or my mounted warriors —
sentences.s538-s10: let no one's heart, full of desire, be at rest — if he is one who enslaves people yet of gentle character.
sentences.s538-s11: This court is wide/open to everyone, both from the ill‑wisher and from the well‑wisher.
sentences.s538-s12: The whole assembly cried 'Bravo!' — 'May the land be prosperous through your justice.'
sentences.s538-s5: For in the world my treasure is justice; the world lives by my fortune and suffering.
sentences.s538-s6: No one can take this treasure from me; ill will comes upon the people from bad deeds.
sentences.s538-s7: When the sovereign is pleased — pure and righteous — this dark earth will harbour no reluctance toward me.
sentences.s538-s8: The whole world, from head to head, is under my protection; approving justice is my way.
sentences.s538-s1: He sat in Baghdad upon the ivory throne; upon his head he set that heart-illuminating crown.
sentences.s538-s2: He girded his waist and took the kings' mace in his hand; having adorned/arranged the seat, he sat in his place.
sentences.s538-s3: They called him Shahanshah; after that, no one recognized him as (one of) Gushtasp.
sentences.s538-s4: When he set the great crown upon his head, he thus made the turquoise throne a memorial.
